meta.title: Further Reflections on Leaving the Eastern Orthodox Church, Part 1
meta.author: Gavin Ortlund (YouTube channel; unnamed guest speaker)

questions.question: What kind of video does the speaker say this is?
options: His first solo video, following a conversation with Gavin Ortlund
A debate hosted by an Orthodox jurisdiction
A panel discussion with multiple church historians
A live Q&A with audience call-ins
questions.answer: His first solo video, following a conversation with Gavin Ortlund

questions.question: Who is the primary audience the speaker says he is aiming toward?
questions.answer: Evangelicals and Protestants

questions.question: What does the speaker say he is NOT trying to do with Orthodox websites, bloggers, or podcasters?
questions.answer: Enter into a back-and-forth debate

questions.question: How does the speaker describe Protestant relation to the ancient church?
options: Protestants must admit they began entirely new communities in the 19th century
The church fathers are irrelevant to Protestant identity
Only Eastern Orthodoxy may claim the church fathers
Organic continuity from the apostolic era is possible even though the church was reformed in the 16th century
questions.answer: Organic continuity from the apostolic era is possible even though the church was reformed in the 16th century

questions.question: Regarding the comment that he does not represent Orthodoxy, what is the speaker’s main response?
options: He agrees it can be fair, but argues no single person or party universally represents Orthodoxy either
He denies that representation matters for any religious tradition
He claims he was the official catechist for his diocese
He says only the patriarch of Constantinople may speak about Orthodoxy
questions.answer: He agrees it can be fair, but argues no single person or party universally represents Orthodoxy either

questions.question: Which pair of ecclesiastical figures does the speaker use to illustrate mutual accusations of not representing Orthodoxy?
options: The Pope of Rome and the Archbishop of Canterbury
The Patriarch of Constantinople and the Patriarch of Moscow
The Coptic Pope and the Armenian Catholicos
questions.answer: The Patriarch of Constantinople and the Patriarch of Moscow

questions.question: Fr. Alexander Schmemann is mentioned as an example of someone whom some Orthodox would describe how?
options: The only theologian all jurisdictions accept without dispute
An innovator who does not represent or articulate Orthodoxy
A figure unknown to modern Orthodox readers
questions.answer: An innovator who does not represent or articulate Orthodoxy

questions.question: Elder Ephraim is used in the talk to illustrate what point?
options: That monasticism is absent from modern Orthodoxy
That all Orthodox seminaries reject Athonite influence
That he was a Protestant reformer
That some priests deny he represents Orthodoxy while other camps insist he does
questions.answer: That some priests deny he represents Orthodoxy while other camps insist he does

questions.question: What question does the speaker pose after listing competing claimants to represent Orthodoxy?
options: How many ecumenical councils are binding?
questions.answer: Who gets to say?

questions.question: How does the speaker characterize the claim that Orthodoxy has the consensus of the fathers?
options: A neutral historical datum requiring no argument
A claim—an argument—not a self-evident given
A scientific measurement of patristic DNA
A legal ruling enforced by a single global synod
questions.answer: A claim—an argument—not a self-evident given

questions.question: Which theologian is tied to the phrase “neo-patristic synthesis” in the talk?
questions.answer: Fr. Georges Florovsky

questions.question: What about Florovsky leads some to say he does not represent Orthodoxy, according to the speaker?
options: He rejected all use of the church fathers
He joined the World Council of Churches
questions.answer: He joined the World Council of Churches

questions.question: What eschatological contrast does the speaker use to complicate talk of a simple patristic consensus?
options: Amillennialism (or close) after Constantine vs premillennialism in many early fathers
questions.answer: Amillennialism (or close) after Constantine vs premillennialism in many early fathers

questions.question: What label does the speaker use for Orthodox unity in this connection?
questions.answer: Paper unity

questions.question: How does the speaker respond to the criticism that he received bad catechesis?
options: He insists his priests were universally heretical
He allows it may be true but thinks his formation was fairly normal—which he finds tragic
He says only Americans face bad catechesis
questions.answer: He allows it may be true but thinks his formation was fairly normal—which he finds tragic

questions.question: Which figures are cited in connection with normative teaching documents that were hard to recover?
options: St. Philaret of Moscow and St. Peter Mogila
questions.answer: St. Philaret of Moscow and St. Peter Mogila

questions.question: St. Isaac of Nineveh is mentioned in relation to which controversial theme?
questions.answer: Universalism

questions.question: Which author’s *The Roots of Christian Mysticism* is named as containing universalist-leaning material?
questions.answer: Olivier Clément

questions.question: Origen is discussed alongside which conciliar judgments?
options: Only the Council of Nicaea I
Anathema at the Council of Trullo and reaffirmation at the Seventh Ecumenical Council
questions.answer: Anathema at the Council of Trullo and reaffirmation at the Seventh Ecumenical Council

questions.question: How does the speaker describe Orthodoxy’s actual state of voice and teaching, despite claims of unity?
options: Uniformity enforced by a single global catechism
questions.answer: A cacophony or chaos

questions.question: What kind of internal Orthodox behavior does the speaker illustrate with the Antiochian example?
options: One Orthodox Christian attacking another for not being “Orthodox enough”
questions.answer: One Orthodox Christian attacking another for not being “Orthodox enough”

questions.question: On penal substitutionary atonement, which patristic and dialogue examples does the speaker cite as evidence it appears in tradition?
options: Chrysostom, Cyril of Alexandria, and Patriarch Jeremiah’s conversations with Lutherans
No fathers ever discussed the cross
questions.answer: Chrysostom, Cyril of Alexandria, and Patriarch Jeremiah’s conversations with Lutherans

questions.question: What related term does the speaker use as part of the same “family” of ideas as penal substitution?
questions.answer: Vicarious satisfaction

questions.question: How does the speaker characterize who “articulates and represents” Orthodoxy in practice?
options: Only seminary deans, never parish priests
A single anonymous committee in Geneva
Anyone who has read the entire Philokalia in Greek
Often whoever is currently defending Orthodoxy, producing contradictory answers from different voices
questions.answer: Often whoever is currently defending Orthodoxy, producing contradictory answers from different voices

questions.question: How does the speaker end this installment?
options: By pausing and promising to pick up another subject from the comments later
By announcing reception into a new jurisdiction that day
By converting to a non-Christian religion
By refusing ever to discuss Orthodoxy again
questions.answer: By pausing and promising to pick up another subject from the comments later
